planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in well-drained, slightly acidic, dry to average, deep soil in full sun; California black oak grows on drier slopes and ridges at 2000–8000 feet elevation throughout the Sierra Nevada and Coast Ranges. The large, deeply lobed leaves turn gold and russet in fall.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; California black oak is the largest and most widely distributed of the California red-group oaks, growing from the Oregon border to Baja California in the Sierra Nevada and Coast Ranges at mid-elevations. The deeply lobed, bristle-tipped leaves (characteristic of the red oak group) are large and bold — up to 8 inches long — turning brilliant gold, orange, and russet in fall for some of the finest fall color of any California native. The large, nutritious acorns were a dietary staple of numerous Sierran indigenous cultures including the Miwok and Paiute, providing a high-calorie starch food after leaching of bitter tannins. California black oak is an important food source for Steller's jays, band-tailed pigeons, acorn woodpeckers, mule deer, and black bears in mountain habitats. A magnificent landscape tree for large-scale California and Pacific Northwest plantings.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; bluff oak is a white oak group species of bluffs, rocky slopes, and dry uplands of the lower Piedmont and Gulf Coastal Plain. It is closely related to Durand oak and often confused with it in the field.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; bluff oak is a medium to large white-oak group oak of the lower Piedmont and Gulf Coastal Plain, naturally growing on rocky bluffs, dry slopes, and upland sites from North Carolina to Georgia and Alabama. The leaves are variable but typically club-shaped with rounded lobes, resembling a smaller or more graceful white oak leaf. Like all white-oak group oaks, the sweet acorns ripen in a single season and are immediately germinated by squirrels and blue jays. The tree provides important acorn mast for wildlife and supports the full suite of moth and butterfly caterpillars that depend on native oaks. Relatively uncommon in cultivation and underused compared to its relatives, bluff oak is a dependable, drought-tolerant native for dry, upland settings in the Gulf South.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1 inch depth in well-drained, rocky, calcareous, dry, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sandpaper oak grows on dry, rocky, limestone slopes and desert grasslands of west Texas and New Mexico where it forms dense, scrubby thickets adapted to poor, alkaline, dry conditions.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, rocky, calcareous, dry, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sandpaper oak is a small, shrubby to tree-like, semi-evergreen oak of dry, rocky, limestone slopes in west Texas and New Mexico, named for the distinctively rough, sandpaper-textured upper surface of the small, leathery, holly-like leaves with spiny teeth. It forms dense, thorny, impenetrable thickets in its natural habitat that serve as important cover for scaled quail, roadrunners, and various lizards in the Chihuahuan Desert. The small acorns provide food for wildlife in an arid region where food sources are sparse. A tough, dry-land shrub-oak for rocky, alkaline, exposed garden conditions in west Texas and the Southwest where most ornamental plants struggle. Extremely drought and heat tolerant once established. Can form a functional barrier hedge in harsh, exposed xeric conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in dry, sandy or rocky, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; false pennyroyal is an annual of dry, sandy, and rocky prairies and open disturbed areas of the central US. It self-sows freely where conditions are appropriate and can establish a self-maintaining colony.

directions: Direct sow in dry, sandy or rocky, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; false pennyroyal is a small, hairy (hispid — reflecting the species name) annual of dry, sandy, and rocky prairies and open areas across the central US, closely related to American pennyroyal (H. pulegioides). The plant has a similar pungent, camphor-mint aroma when crushed and has been used in similar ways as a mild insect repellent and flavoring herb. The small, lavender-blue, tubular flowers are produced throughout summer and are visited by small native bees. Like American pennyroyal, the plant self-sows freely in appropriate dry, lean, sandy conditions and can be left to naturalize in a dry wildflower or prairie garden. More common in the Great Plains and Midwest than eastern pennyroyal, occupying similar dry, disturbed, sandy-soil habitats.

planting.note: Plant divisions or direct sow in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; needlegrass rush grows naturally in shallow water margins, wet meadows, seeps, and ditches across most of North America. It is one of the most widespread native rushes in the US.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in shallow water margins, wet meadows, and rain garden wet zones; needlegrass rush (tapered rush) is one of the most widespread and common native rushes of North America, growing naturally in a broad range of wet habitats from coastal marshes to mountain seeps. The stems bear distinctive, rounded, terminal flower/seed clusters that look like small brown spheres when mature. The seed heads are eaten by various marsh birds and shorebirds. Fine-textured and relatively short compared to soft rush, it naturalizes readily in appropriate wet conditions to create fine, tuft-forming groundcover in wet areas. An excellent native filler for the wet margins of bioswales, rain gardens, and restored wetlands where its widespread adaptability across soil types and moisture levels makes it one of the more reliable wetland natives for mitigation planting.
